title: WMG SSH Weak Password Attack
description: Step into the role of a junior penetration tester at WMG and prove that a weak SSH password on an internal server can be found, cracked and used to gain access -- then learn how to stop it.
prerequisites: Basic familiarity with the Linux command line
Understanding of what SSH is used for
outcomes: You will learn how to scan a server for open services with nmap
You will learn how to run an SSH dictionary attack with hydra
You will understand why weak passwords are dangerous and how to defend against brute-force attacks

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information: please read carefully

* The solution of a level **will be required** in the further levels. Do not try to skip ahead. If you reveal the solution to a level, complete all of the steps anyway -- you will need the result later on.
* If you get stuck during the game, feel free to use Google or the built-in `--help` of any tool.
* This game is for educational purposes only. Unauthorized use of the tools incorporated into this game is illegal. The story is fictitious, and every action you take stays inside the isolated training sandbox.

# Storyline: The Intern's Account

Welcome to Warwick Manufacturing Group. It is your first Monday as a junior penetration tester on the WMG security team, and the coffee has barely gone cold before your manager drops a ticket on your desk.

"We think we have a problem," she says. "Last week the sysadmin was buried under a mountain of work. A new summer intern was starting, and the sysadmin needed to get them productive fast, so a fresh account was thrown together on one of our internal servers. You know how it goes -- *'I'll set a proper password later.'* Later never happened. Now the security team is nervous that account is a wide-open door, and I need someone to find out before somebody outside these walls does."

She slides a Kali Linux workstation across the desk. Kali is the penetration tester's toolkit of choice: it ships with everything you need for a job like this. `nmap` will map out which services the server is exposing. `hydra` will hammer a login with a list of common passwords until one sticks. `ssh` will let you walk through the door once you have the key. A wordlist of the passwords people lazily reuse -- `password`, `qwerty`, `letmein`, and hundreds more -- is already waiting in your home directory.

Your mission, should you accept it (and you already have -- you signed the engagement letter), is to think like an attacker. You will approach the target from the outside, with no inside knowledge beyond a rumour about a hastily created intern account. First you will confirm the server is reachable and find out what it is running. Then you will work out who to target. Then you will crack the password, log in, and recover the proof: a flag file that only the account owner can read.

The target sits at `10.1.27.10`. Your Kali attack box is at `10.1.27.23`. Stay inside the sandbox, trust your tools, and prove the hole is real.

Good luck, analyst. The clock is running.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
# Connect to your attacker machine

You will run this entire engagement from a **Kali Linux** attacker machine located at `10.1.27.23`.

Open a terminal and connect to it over SSH using the credentials provided by the training platform (they are shown in the sandbox access details):

```
ssh <username>@10.1.27.23
```

Once you are logged in you have everything you need. Kali ships with `nmap`, `hydra` and `ssh`, and a wordlist of common passwords has already been placed in your home directory at `/home/kali/wordlist.txt`.

Your one and only target for this engagement is the internal server at `10.1.27.10`. Do **not** scan, probe or attack anything outside this sandbox -- doing so is both against the rules of engagement and, in the real world, illegal.

When you are connected to the Kali machine and ready to begin, submit the passkey **`start`** to continue.
levels.local_content: You will be working from a Kali Linux attacker machine at `10.1.27.23`. Open a terminal on it. Your target server is the internal machine at `10.1.27.10`, and a wordlist has been placed at `/home/kali/wordlist.txt`. Do not attack anything outside the sandbox. When you are ready, submit the passkey `start` to continue.

levels.title: Reconnaissance -- Is SSH Running?
levels.content:
Every engagement begins with reconnaissance. Before you can attack a service, you have to prove it is actually there -- attacking a port that is closed just wastes your time and makes noise for nothing.

Your manager's ticket mentions a login account, which means the server almost certainly exposes a remote-login service. On Linux machines that service is nearly always **SSH** (Secure Shell). From your Kali machine, scan the target at `10.1.27.10` and find out which ports are open and what is listening on them.

Look carefully through the scan output for the line describing the remote-login service. You need the port it is listening on.

The flag for this level is the port number on which the SSH service is running on the target server.
levels.solution:
Scan the target with `nmap`:

```
kali@attacker:~$ nmap 10.1.27.10

Starting Nmap ( https://nmap.org )
Nmap scan report for 10.1.27.10
Host is up.

PORT   STATE SERVICE
22/tcp open  ssh
```

The `ssh` service is listening on port `22`, so the flag is **22**.

hints.title: Which tool?
hints.content: Nmap is the industry-standard network scanner. It shows open ports and the services running behind them.

hints.title: What to look for
hints.content: Look for a line containing `ssh` in the output. The number before `/tcp` on that line is the port you want.

levels.title: Intelligence -- Who is the Target User?
levels.content:
You have confirmed SSH is alive on port `22`. But a login attack needs two things: a password *and* a username. Hammering the login with the wrong username will get you nowhere no matter how good your wordlist is, so a smart attacker gathers intelligence first.

Go back to the briefing. Your manager told you the account at the heart of this engagement was hurriedly provisioned for a **new summer intern**. Sysadmins under pressure rarely invent creative usernames -- they reach for the obvious one that describes the person's role. Think about what a WMG IT department would most naturally call a temporary training account for someone who has just arrived to learn.

The flag for this level is the username you will target in the attack.
levels.solution:
This level is about deduction, not a command. The briefing describes the account as one created for a **new summer intern** -- someone who has arrived to learn. A rushed sysadmin would give such a temporary, role-based account the most obvious possible name. The username is **student**.

You will use this username in the next level as the `-l` argument to `hydra`.

hints.title: Think about it
hints.content: What username would an IT department give to a brand-new intern who has arrived to learn? Re-read the briefing -- the answer is a single, common word.

hints.title: Common naming conventions
hints.content: Most sysadmins name an account after the person's role rather than inventing something clever. For someone here to learn, the role is very straightforward.

levels.title: Attack -- Brute Force the Password
levels.content:
You now have everything you need to launch the attack: the service (`SSH` on port `22`), the target (`10.1.27.10`) and the username (`student`). The only thing missing is the password -- and guessing one attempt at a time by hand would take forever.

Instead, automate it with a **dictionary attack**. A wordlist of common, lazy passwords has already been placed on your Kali machine at `/home/kali/wordlist.txt`. Use `hydra` to try every password in that list against the `student` account over SSH until one of them lets you in.

Watch the output: when hydra finds a match it will print the host, the login and the password on a single line.

The flag for this level is the password you recover for the `student` account.
levels.solution:
Run a dictionary attack against the `student` account over SSH using the supplied wordlist:

```
kali@attacker:~$ hydra -l student -P /home/kali/wordlist.txt ssh://10.1.27.10

Hydra starting...
[DATA] attacking ssh://10.1.27.10:22/
[22][ssh] host: 10.1.27.10   login: student   password: password123
1 of 1 target successfully completed, 1 valid password found
```

Hydra tried each password from the wordlist and found a match. The recovered password is **password123**.

hints.title: Which tool?
hints.content: Hydra is a fast password-cracking tool that supports SSH, and it is already installed on Kali.

hints.title: Hydra syntax
hints.content: The general form is `hydra -l <username> -P <wordlist> ssh://<target>`. Use `-l` for a single known username and `-P` for the wordlist file.

levels.title: Access -- Log In and Retrieve the Flag
levels.content:
This is the moment that turns a theory into proof. You have valid credentials -- `student` / `password123` -- recovered by cracking a weak password from the outside. Now use them.

Log in to the target server at `10.1.27.10` as the `student` user with the password you just cracked. Accept the host key if SSH asks about it, and enter the password when prompted. Once you land on the box, look around the account's home directory: there is a flag file waiting for you that only the `student` user is allowed to read -- which is exactly why gaining this account mattered.

Read that file and submit its contents. This is the evidence your manager needs.

The flag for this level is the contents of the flag file.
levels.solution:
Log in with the cracked credentials and read the flag from the home directory:

```
kali@attacker:~$ ssh student@10.1.27.10
The authenticity of host '10.1.27.10' can't be established.
Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])? yes
student@10.1.27.10's password: password123

student@server:~$ ls
flag.txt  wordlist.txt

student@server:~$ cat flag.txt
WMG{ssh_w3ak_p4ssw0rds_are_never_ok}
```

The flag is **WMG{ssh_w3ak_p4ssw0rds_are_never_ok}**. You have proven that a weak password gave a total stranger a real shell on an internal server.

hints.title: SSH syntax
hints.content: `ssh username@hostname` -- use the `student` username and the password you cracked in the previous level. Answer `yes` if it asks about the host authenticity.

hints.title: Finding the flag
hints.content: Once you are logged in, run `ls` to see the files in the home directory, then `cat flag.txt` to read the flag.

levels.title: Debrief -- What Did You Learn?
levels.instructions: Answer these questions to demonstrate your understanding of the attack you just performed and how a defender would stop it.

choices.text: Scans networks for open ports and services

questions.text: Why was the password easy to crack?

choices.text: The password appeared in a common wordlist

questions.text: Which MITRE ATT&CK tactic does Hydra implement in this attack?
What is the most effective fix for this vulnerability?

choices.text: Enforce strong passwords and use SSH key authentication

questions.text: In one sentence, explain why 'password123' is a weak password.
